topic.title: HTML Core Image Techniques
topic.description: HTML image techniques control how images load, scale, and adapt across devices using src, alt, srcset, and picture.

content.text: Images in HTML: Embedding, Accessibility, and Responsiveness

text.value: element is the foundation of all HTML image embedding. At minimum it requires a
attribute pointing to the image resource and an
attribute providing a text alternative. Beyond the basics, the
attributes let the browser choose the most appropriate image resolution for the user's screen density and viewport, and the
element enables serving entirely different image files based on media conditions or format support. The
attribute defers off-screen image loading until the user scrolls near them, and
allows the browser to decode the image off the main thread. All image techniques are defined in the

rows: URL of the image resource to load
Text alternative for screen readers and broken image fallback
Reserve layout space, prevent Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS)
List of image candidates at different resolutions or widths
Tells browser how wide the image will be rendered at each breakpoint
When using srcset with w descriptors
Defers loading until image is near the viewport
Decodes image off the main thread to avoid jank
Container for art direction and format fallbacks
For art direction or format negotiation
Candidate inside picture with media or type conditions

content.title: Always Set width and height on img
content.text: Omitting width and height causes the browser to allocate zero space for the image until it loads, then reflow the page when dimensions are known. This Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) is a Core Web Vitals failure. Set both attributes to the image's natural dimensions even when CSS scales it.

examples.title: Production-Ready img with All Performance and Accessibility Attributes
examples.description: A single img element demonstrating every recommended attribute in combination: alt for accessibility, width and height for CLS prevention, loading and decoding for performance, and srcset for resolution switching.
examples.output: The browser renders a full-width image of hikers on a mountain trail inside a figure element with a small grey caption below it. The image scales to the container width while maintaining its 3:2 aspect ratio. No layout shift occurs as the page loads because the browser reserved the correct space using the width and height attributes. On a Retina display the browser selects the 1600w variant from srcset automatically.

examples.title: WRONG vs RIGHT: Common img Mistakes
examples.description: Contrasts four common img mistakes against their correct counterparts, covering missing alt, missing dimensions, wrong lazy loading on hero images, and decorative image handling.
examples.output: All WRONG versions may display identically in the browser but fail in different ways. WRONG 1 causes screen readers to announce the filename. WRONG 2 causes a jarring layout jump when the image loads. WRONG 3 delays the hero image and hurts Lighthouse LCP scores. WRONG 4 interrupts screen reader users with an irrelevant announcement. The RIGHT versions solve each issue without changing the visual appearance.

examples.title: figure and figcaption for Contextual Images
examples.description: Demonstrates the correct pattern for images that need a visible caption, using figure and figcaption to associate the caption semantically with the image rather than using a nearby paragraph.
examples.output: Two image cards render with a thin border and rounded corners. Each has the image on top and a light grey caption bar at the bottom. Screen readers announce the alt text of each image followed by the figcaption text, treating each figure as a self-contained unit with both image description and editorial context.

text.value: when serving the same image at multiple resolutions to let the browser pick the most efficient size for the user's device and screen density.

attribute is like the caption on a photograph in a newspaper. If you remove the photo, the caption tells you exactly what was there. A good alt text gives the same information as the image itself, not a description of what an image looks like.
as a menu and an order form.
is the menu of available image sizes.

is the order form telling the browser how large the image will appear at each viewport width. The browser reads both and selects the most efficient option, the way a waiter picks the right portion based on your order.
element is like a casting call with a fallback understudy. Each
is a candidate that auditions in order. The browser picks the first one it can support. If none qualify, the
fallback steps in, ensuring something always appears regardless of browser capability.
delays the LCP element and directly lowers Lighthouse performance scores.
Descriptive alt on decorative images: giving a purely decorative
causes screen readers to announce irrelevant content, cluttering the audio experience.

to hint to the browser that this image is the highest-priority resource on the page. Combined with
before deploying. AVIF typically achieves 50 percent smaller file sizes than JPEG at equivalent visual quality. Pair the modern format with a JPEG fallback inside
to support all browsers without serving oversized files to modern ones.

subtopics.title: Alt Attribute Usage
subtopics.description: The alt attribute provides a text alternative for images, required for screen readers and broken image fallback.

text.value: element without exception, though its value depends on the image's purpose. For
the value should describe the content or function of the image as concisely as possible. For
that add no information, the value must be an
) so screen readers skip it entirely. For
such as icon-only buttons, the alt text should describe the action, not the icon appearance. Omitting the
attribute entirely (not the same as empty) causes screen readers to fall back to reading the filename, which is a

rows: alt="Bar chart showing 40% growth in Q3 revenue"
Short alt plus aria-describedby to long description
alt="Sales funnel diagram" + nearby paragraph with full data

examples.title: Alt Text Patterns for Every Image Type
examples.description: Shows the correct alt value for five image categories: informational, decorative, functional, image of text, and a complex chart with an extended description pattern.
examples.code_snippet: North America led with $131,700, followed by East at $175,500, West at $141,200, and South at $90,500. East showed the strongest quarter-over-quarter growth at 12%.
examples.output: Five images render on the page. The team photo shows a group of people; screen readers announce the full team description. The wave divider is completely skipped by screen readers. Clicking the logo navigates to the homepage; screen readers announce 'Acme Corp homepage, link'. The promo banner announces the offer text verbatim. The chart announces its title then reads the extended description paragraph for full data context.

text.value: as the radio broadcast description of a scene in a television show. A viewer who cannot see the screen still needs to understand what is happening. A good alt text communicates the meaning, not just the visual appearance. Writing 'a person' tells nothing; 'Sarah presenting the Q3 results to the board' tells the full story.
is an intentional signal to screen readers that this image is purely decorative and carries no information. It is not laziness; it is a deliberate instruction. Omitting
entirely sends no signal at all and forces the reader to guess, usually resulting in the filename being announced.

text by asking: 'if this image failed to load, what text would give the user the same information?' For a chart, that means the key insight or data summary, not 'bar chart image'. Run your page through the

subtopics.title: Responsive Image Techniques (srcset, sizes)
subtopics.description: The srcset and sizes attributes let browsers select the most efficient image candidate for the user's screen and viewport.

text.value: attribute accepts a comma-separated list of image candidates. Each candidate is a URL followed by either a
) indicating the image's pixel width, or a
). Width descriptors are preferred for responsive layouts because the browser combines them with the
attribute to calculate the optimal candidate before the CSS layout is known. The

attribute is a comma-separated list of media condition and slot-width pairs. The browser evaluates them top to bottom and uses the width from the first matching condition to look up the best candidate in
. If no condition matches, the last value (without a media condition) is the default. The
attribute always serves as the final fallback for browsers that do not support

content.title: The browser decides, not you
content.text: With srcset and sizes you provide a menu of options and hints. The browser makes the final selection based on screen density, current network conditions, and its cache. This means you cannot force the browser to pick a specific candidate, and that is intentional: the browser has information about the user's conditions that your HTML does not.

examples.title: srcset with Width Descriptors and sizes for Responsive Layout
examples.description: A complete responsive image implementation using width descriptors in srcset and a multi-breakpoint sizes declaration, showing how the browser calculates which image to download.
examples.code_snippet:
Trail Reports

srcset=" /images/trail-a-400.jpg 400w, /images/trail-a-800.jpg 800w, /images/trail-a-1600.jpg 1600w " sizes=" (max-width: 640px) 100vw, (max-width: 1024px) calc(50vw - 1.5rem), calc(33vw - 1.5rem) " alt="Rocky alpine trail winding through sparse pine trees at 2000m elevation" width="800" height="533" loading="lazy" decoding="async" />

Alpine Trail A — Difficulty: Moderate
examples.output: The page shows a responsive card grid. On a phone at 390px the image fills the full card width. On a tablet two cards appear side by side. On desktop three cards appear per row. In each case the browser downloads only the image variant that matches the rendered size and screen density, avoiding unnecessarily large downloads on small screens.

text.value: as a restaurant menu of the same dish in different portion sizes (400w, 800w, 1600w). The

attribute is your pre-order that tells the kitchen how hungry you will be when you arrive. The kitchen (browser) then prepares exactly the right portion before you even sit down, avoiding waste in both directions.
are like offering a standard print and a high-resolution print of the same photo. Width descriptors (
) are more powerful because they let the browser factor in both screen density and rendered image size simultaneously, making a much smarter selection.
Use the Chrome DevTools Network tab with throttling set to 'Fast 3G' and device emulation enabled to verify which
candidate the browser selects. Check the 'Initiator' column for image requests to see the resolved
value. If the wrong candidate is chosen, your
declaration does not match the actual rendered width of the image in CSS.

subtopics.title: Picture Element Usage
subtopics.description: The picture element enables art direction and format negotiation by offering multiple source candidates with conditions.

text.value: element is a container that holds one or more
in source order and uses the first one whose
condition matches the current viewport and whose
MIME type is supported. It is used for two distinct purposes:
, where a differently cropped or composed image is served at different viewport sizes using the
, where a modern format such as
is served to capable browsers using the
attribute, with a JPEG or PNG fallback for older ones. The
attributes must always be on the fallback

content.title: Order sources from most to least specific
content.text: List source elements from most restrictive condition to least restrictive. The browser picks the first match, so a broad media condition listed before a narrow one will always win. For format negotiation, list AVIF before WebP before the img fallback, and for art direction list the most narrow breakpoint first.

examples.title: picture for Format Negotiation and Art Direction Combined
examples.description: Demonstrates picture used for both purposes simultaneously: serving AVIF to capable browsers, WebP to those without AVIF support, and cropped portrait versions for mobile versus landscape versions for desktop.
examples.code_snippet:
Format Negotiation (AVIF > WebP > JPEG)

Art Direction (Portrait on Mobile, Landscape on Desktop)
examples.output: On a modern desktop browser, the first picture element serves the AVIF file if supported, otherwise WebP, otherwise JPEG. The second picture element shows a wide landscape kitchen photo across the full container width. On a narrow mobile screen, the same chef image switches to a tightly cropped portrait orientation that fills the screen without showing empty counter space. The fallback img ensures the image appears on all browsers including Internet Explorer.

text.value: candidates is like a casting director who auditions actors in priority order. The first actor who fits the role (matches the media condition and format support) gets the part. If no one qualifies, the understudy (
fallback) steps in to ensure the show goes on.

is like a magazine editor choosing a different crop of the same photograph for the print cover versus the website thumbnail. The subject is the same but the framing is intentionally adapted to the canvas size.
alone cannot do this because it always serves the same composition at different resolutions.
When combining art direction and format negotiation in the same

condition. List AVIF sources before WebP sources at each breakpoint. The browser evaluates both attributes simultaneously and selects the first source where both the media condition and the type are supported.

seo_metadata.meta_title: HTML Core Image Techniques | alt, srcset, sizes, and picture Guide
seo_metadata.meta_description: Learn HTML image techniques including alt text, srcset, sizes, and the picture element for accessible, responsive, and performant images on any device.
